one_line_takeaway: A Preamble-authored survey that unifies prompt injection into a delivery/modality/propagation taxonomy and argues that 'hybrid' prompt-injection-plus-web-exploit attacks (XSS/CSRF/SQLi, AI worms) defeat traditional controls, calling for layered AI-native defenses — conceptually useful but empirically thin and self-referential.
key_finding: Prompt injection has evolved from isolated text manipulation into hybrid cyber-AI threats where an injected prompt turns a trusted AI into a payload generator, systematically defeating traditional web defenses (WAFs, XSS filters, CSP, CSRF tokens, ORM/parameterized-query safeguards) because those controls trust AI output and were built for static, predictable inputs. The authors argue no single defense — traditional or AI-specific — suffices in isolation, and advocate a layered, AI-native architecture combining input classification/token tagging, architectural control/data-flow isolation (CaMeL), and spotlighting of untrusted content. The paper's persuasive quantitative anchor is borrowed from CaMeL [10], not measured here: CaMeL solves 77% of AgentDojo tasks WITH security guarantees versus 84% for an undefended system, showing strong defense at modest utility cost.
core_contribution: A survey/position paper ('Prompt Injection 2.0') by Preamble, Inc. that (1) proposes a unified three-dimensional taxonomy of prompt injection threats organized by delivery vector (direct vs. indirect injection), attack modality (multimodal, code, and hybrid injection), and propagation behavior (recursive injection, autonomous AI worms); and (2) analyzes how prompt injection converges with classical web-application exploits (XSS, CSRF, SQL injection) to form 'hybrid' cyber-AI threats that evade both traditional web controls and AI-specific defenses. It is a conceptual synthesis and threat-taxonomy paper, not an empirical study — it contributes no new experiments, benchmarks, or measured attack-success rates of its own. Its defense discussion is a layered-architecture recommendation that combines three EXTERNALLY-developed mechanisms (Preamble's own patented trusted/untrusted token tagging, CaMeL's architectural isolation, and Hines et al.'s spotlighting).
threat_model: Adversary supplies malicious natural-language instructions to an LLM-integrated or agentic AI system, either directly (in the user prompt) or indirectly (embedded in external content the agent processes: web pages, PDFs/emails, database rows, API responses, images, audio/video transcripts). The paper emphasizes agentic settings where LLMs autonomously call tools/APIs and coordinate with other agents, so a single injection can escalate to account takeover, remote code execution, unauthorized SQL/state-changing operations, data exfiltration, or self-propagating infection across an agent network. The distinguishing threat is 'hybrid': the injected prompt causes the trusted AI to emit a payload (e.g., JavaScript, SQL) that downstream security controls whitelist precisely because it originates from the trusted AI, bypassing WAFs, XSS filters, CSP, CSRF tokens, and input sanitizers designed for predictable, human-supplied attack patterns.
limitations: It is a non-empirical survey/position paper with clear conflict-of-interest framing: authored by Preamble, Inc., it repeatedly centers Preamble's own 2022 discovery, its US Patent 12,118,471 B2 mitigation, and its 2024 guardrail framework as foundational, giving the 'related work' a promotional cast. The paper itself concedes 'there are limited studies on hybrid threats' attack success rates in the wild,' and it reports no original success rates, no reproducible evaluation, and no comparison of its recommended layered stack against alternatives. Several claims rest on non-peer-reviewed blog posts (Embrace The Red) and a news article (Nikkei). The proposed defense is a conceptual composition of three separately-published mechanisms with no integrated implementation or benchmark demonstrating they actually compose. Note for graph ingestion: the 77%/84% AgentDojo figures and the 'near-zero white-box attack success rate' belong to CaMeL and Yi et al. respectively — they are cited results, not this paper's contribution.

quantitative_results: CaMeL (cited work [10], NOT this paper's own result): solves 77% of AgentDojo benchmark tasks with formal security guarantees, versus 84% for an undefended system

Yi et al. BIPIA (cited [14]): all evaluated LLMs were vulnerable to indirect prompt injection, with more capable models paradoxically showing higher attack success rates in text-based scenarios; their white-box defenses achieved near-zero attack success rate while preserving legitimate-task performance
This paper reports NO original quantitative results, attack-success rates, or measurements of its own
baselines_compared: No experimental baselines — the paper is a qualitative survey. Traditional controls discussed as failing (not benchmarked): Web Application Firewalls (WAFs), XSS/signature-based filters, Content Security Policy, CSRF tokens, input sanitizers, parameterized queries/ORM safeguards. Defenses recommended (cited, not compared empirically): Preamble token-tagging/classifier mitigation [2], CaMeL architectural isolation [10], spotlighting [29]
